question: In a Kafka-style partitioned log, what is message ordering guaranteed across?
options: Globally across the entire topic
Only within a single partition
Across all partitions of a consumer group
Only for messages with the same timestamp
explanation: Order is guaranteed only within a partition; to keep an entity's events ordered you route them to the same partition via a partition key.

question: What primarily makes the append-only commit log on disk so fast for writes?
options: It uses random-access writes spread across the disk
It compresses every message with a slow codec
It uses sequential disk I/O that is friendly to the page cache
It keeps all messages in RAM and never persists them
explanation: Appending is sequential disk I/O, far faster than random writes, and it works well with the OS page cache plus zero-copy (sendfile) reads.

question: With producer setting acks=all and min.insync.replicas configured, when is a message considered committed?
options: As soon as the leader writes it to its local log
Once it is written to all replicas in the in-sync replica (ISR) set
Once any single follower acknowledges it
As soon as the producer sends it, without waiting
explanation: With acks=all a message is committed only after all ISR members have written it; consumers only ever see committed messages up to the high-watermark.

question: Why does Kafka use pull (with long-polling) rather than pushing messages to consumers?
options: Push offers lower latency in every scenario so pull is purely a fallback
Pull lets consumers control rate and batching, gives natural backpressure, and makes replay trivial
Pull guarantees exactly-once delivery automatically
Push is impossible over TCP connections
explanation: Pull gives consumers control over fetch rate and batching, provides natural backpressure, and makes replaying old offsets trivial; long-poll removes busy-waiting when idle.

question: What is the pragmatic recommended way to achieve exactly-once effect for most consumers?
options: Switch to at-most-once delivery and accept occasional loss
At-least-once delivery plus idempotent consumers that dedupe on a message id
Disable replication so messages can never be redelivered
Use acks=0 so producers never retry
explanation: Default to at-least-once delivery and make processing idempotent (dedupe on message id / use upserts); reach for transactional exactly-once only when duplicates are truly unacceptable.
